children.name: Salmo
children.description: Atlantic salmon and brown trout — the two most culturally significant fish in northern European freshwaters. The Atlantic salmon's life cycle is one of nature's great dramas: born in cold, fast-flowing rivers, the juveniles smolt and migrate to sea, spending 1–4 years feeding in the open North Atlantic before returning with extraordinary precision to the exact river and often the exact stretch where they were born. This natal homing uses a combination of magnetic field sensing and smell — they can detect infinitesimally small concentrations of the specific mineral and biological signature of their home stream. Swedish salmon rivers like Mörrumsån and Lagan are among the most celebrated sport fishing destinations in Europe.

children.name: Salmo salar
children.description: Once incredibly abundant in Swedish rivers including the Göta älv and Dal älv; virtually eliminated from many by hydropower dams in the 20th century. Wild populations are now conservation priorities. Farmed salmon is now one of Sweden's most consumed foods. Males develop a hooked jaw (kype) during breeding season.

children.name: Salmo trutta
children.description: The most widespread salmonid in Europe and a fish of extraordinary ecological flexibility — some populations are purely freshwater residents (öring), others migrate to sea (havsöring). Sea trout of the Baltic are larger, stronger, and darker-fleshed than their freshwater forms. Extensively introduced worldwide and considered invasive in parts of the southern hemisphere.

children.name: Salmo marmoratus
children.description: A distinctive trout of the Adriatic basin in Slovenia and Italy, named for its marbled vermiculated pattern. One of Europe's largest trout, historically exceeding 20 kg; threatened by hybridisation with introduced brown trout.

children.name: Salmo ferox
children.description: A large, long-lived piscivorous ecomorph of brown trout found in deep oligotrophic lakes of the British Isles and Scandinavia, feeding on Arctic char. Can exceed 10 kg and live over 20 years.

children.name: Salmo obtusirostris
children.description: An archaic relict salmonid endemic to a few Adriatic-basin rivers of the Balkans, with a distinctive soft, downturned mouth. Considered a living fossil; IUCN Endangered due to hybridisation and habitat loss.

children.name: Salmo carpio
children.description: A trout endemic to Italy's Lake Garda, spawning at great depth. Critically Endangered due to overfishing and competition with introduced species; one of Europe's most threatened salmonids.

children.name: Salvelinus
children.description: Char — cold-adapted salmonids of glacial lakes and Arctic rivers, often the only fish present in the deepest, coldest, most oligotrophic Scandinavian lakes. The Arctic char (röding) is Sweden's most prized freshwater fish by those who know it — its flesh is salmon-pink and richly flavoured from the cold, clear waters it inhabits. Char populations have been isolated in deep Swedish lakes since the last Ice Age 10,000 years ago and have diverged into dozens of distinct local forms ('ecomorphs') that differ in body shape, feeding habits, and timing — a living laboratory of rapid evolution. Sweden has more distinct Arctic char populations than any other country.

children.name: Salvelinus alpinus
children.description: Sweden's most ecologically diverse freshwater fish, existing in hundreds of genetically and morphologically distinct lake populations. The deepest-dwelling form in Sweden's Lake Vättern reaches 100 m. Intensely red-orange on the belly during spawning — one of the most beautiful freshwater fish in the world.

children.name: Salvelinus fontinalis
children.description: Native to eastern North America; widely introduced to European mountain streams as a sport fish. Its stunning colouration — crimson spots ringed with blue on an olive-green body — makes it arguably the most beautiful freshwater fish in the world. Hybridises with brown trout to produce 'tiger trout'.

children.name: Salvelinus namaycush
children.description: The largest char, native to cold deep lakes of northern North America and reaching over 30 kg. A slow-growing, long-lived top predator; populations were devastated in the Great Lakes by sea lamprey and overfishing before restoration efforts.

children.name: Salvelinus umbla
children.description: A deep-lake char of the Alpine lakes of central Europe, sometimes treated as a form of Arctic char. Prized in Alpine cuisine; populations are sensitive to eutrophication and warming.

children.name: Salvelinus leucomaenis
children.description: A char of Japan, the Russian Far East and Korea, known in Japanese as iwana. Inhabits cold mountain streams and exists in both resident and sea-run forms.

children.name: Salvelinus malma
children.description: A widespread char of the North Pacific rim named after a brightly dressed Dickens character, with vivid spotting. Anadromous and resident forms occur; ecologically important in salmon-bearing rivers where it feeds on eggs.

children.name: Thymallus
children.description: Grayling — elegant, sail-finned salmonids of fast-flowing, cold, clear rivers across the Northern Hemisphere. The European grayling (harr) is distinguished by its enormous, colourful dorsal fin — used in courtship displays — and its characteristic thyme-like smell when fresh (the genus name means 'smelling of thyme'). Grayling are regarded by many as the most beautiful freshwater fish in Europe and are fastidiously selective fly-fishing targets. They are stream quality indicators: grayling require cold, clean, well-oxygenated water and disappear from rivers that warm or accumulate silt. Their presence signals a high-quality watercourse.

children.name: Thymallus thymallus
children.description: Sweden's most elegant stream fish. Found in cold, fast rivers from southern Sweden to Lapland. Its large, sail-like dorsal fin is used in courtship — males erect it to display iridescent purple and green colouration. An excellent indicator of river health.

children.name: Thymallus arcticus
children.description: The grayling of Siberia and northern North America. Montana's state fish — extirpated from the lower 48 states by early 20th century but persisting in Montana and Alaska. Will rise to a dry fly with spectacular leaps.

children.name: Oncorhynchus
children.description: Pacific salmon and rainbow trout — the most ecologically important fish of the Pacific Rim. Pacific salmon are semelparous: after a single spawning migration, every individual dies. The decaying carcasses of millions of salmon returning to Pacific rivers fertilise the surrounding forest with marine-derived nitrogen and phosphorus — nutrients carried hundreds of kilometres inland by bears, eagles, and ravens. This marine-to-terrestrial nutrient subsidy is so significant that scientists can trace it in the growth rings of trees kilometres from any river. Rainbow trout (regnbåge) is widely farmed and has been introduced to rivers across Sweden and Europe.

children.name: Oncorhynchus mykiss
children.description: The world's most widely farmed and sport-fished salmonid. Introduced to Sweden and dozens of other countries for aquaculture and sport fishing; can hybridise with and displace native brown trout. Sea-run form ('steelhead') is considered a different fish in ecology and behaviour.

children.name: Oncorhynchus nerka
children.description: The only Pacific salmon that obligately rears in a freshwater lake before going to sea. Spawning males turn vivid red with green heads — the most dramatically coloured of all Pacific salmon. Some runs in Alaska still number in the tens of millions.

children.name: Oncorhynchus tshawytscha
children.description: The largest Pacific salmon, reaching 61 kg. Called 'king salmon' in Alaska. Its fat-rich flesh is the basis of much of the Pacific Northwest's fishing industry. Introduced to New Zealand where it runs in South Island rivers — far from its native range.

children.name: Oncorhynchus kisutch
children.description: A medium-sized Pacific salmon prized by anglers for its acrobatic fights. Spawning males turn bright red on the flanks; juveniles rear in streams for a year before going to sea. Several southern populations are threatened.

children.name: Oncorhynchus gorbuscha
children.description: The smallest and most abundant Pacific salmon, with a strict two-year life cycle. Spawning males develop a pronounced hump. Has colonised rivers in northern Europe after introductions to Russia, becoming invasive in Scandinavia.

children.name: Oncorhynchus keta
children.description: A Pacific salmon with the widest natural range, spawning in lower river reaches. Spawning fish develop striking purple and green tiger-striped flanks. A major commercial species, especially for its roe (ikura).

children.name: Oncorhynchus clarkii
children.description: A western North American trout named for the red-orange slash marks under the jaw, with many subspecies adapted to distinct watersheds. Several subspecies are threatened by hybridisation and habitat loss.

children.name: Oncorhynchus apache
children.description: A golden-yellow trout endemic to the White Mountains of Arizona and Arizona's state fish. Once near extinction, it has recovered through conservation on tribal lands and is a US recovery success story.

children.name: Oncorhynchus aguabonita
children.description: A brilliantly coloured trout native to icy headwater streams of California's Sierra Nevada and the California state freshwater fish. Renowned for its golden-yellow flanks and red lateral stripe; threatened by hybridisation.

children.name: Coregonus
children.description: Whitefishes and ciscoes — silvery, plankton- and bottom-feeding salmonids of cold northern lakes and rivers. Coregonus is one of the most taxonomically complex fish genera, with numerous lake-endemic forms that have radiated rapidly since the last Ice Age. Many forms are commercially important and several have gone extinct through eutrophication.

children.name: Coregonus lavaretus
children.description: A widespread and morphologically variable whitefish (sik) of northern European lakes and rivers, important in Scandinavian commercial and subsistence fisheries. Its roe is a prized delicacy.

children.name: Coregonus albula
children.description: A small, slender, planktivorous whitefish whose roe (löjrom) from the Gulf of Bothnia is a celebrated Swedish delicacy with protected designation. Forms huge schools in cold northern lakes.

children.name: Coregonus clupeaformis
children.description: A commercially vital deepwater whitefish of North American lakes and the backbone of Great Lakes fisheries. Bottom-feeds on invertebrates; populations are affected by invasive mussels altering food webs.

children.name: Hucho
children.description: Taimens and huchen — enormous, elongate, predatory salmonids of Eurasian rivers, among the largest freshwater fish of the salmon family. Apex river predators that hunt fish, amphibians and even small mammals; all species are threatened by damming, overfishing and habitat degradation.

children.name: Hucho hucho
children.description: A large predatory salmonid endemic to the Danube basin, reaching 1.5 m and over 30 kg. IUCN Endangered, threatened by dams and pollution; the focus of major reintroduction efforts in central Europe.

children.name: Hucho taimen
children.description: The world's largest salmonid, reaching over 2 m and 100 kg, inhabiting cold rivers across Siberia and Mongolia. A long-lived apex predator and renowned catch-and-release sport fish; IUCN Vulnerable due to overfishing.
